sections.title: B Background and Technical Choices
sections.content: B.1 Background The evolution of visual synthesis has rapidly transitioned from isolated generative tasks to integrated multimodal frameworks. This section reviews the foundational trajectories contextualizing our work: text-toimage diffusion, controllable editing, vision-language models (VLMs), and the recent paradigm shift towards unified generation. Text-to-Image Diffusion Models. Diffusion models [56] have fundamentally revolutionized visual synthesis. Foundational works like LDM [107] and DALL-E 2 [104] established the paradigm of high-fidelity textconditioned generation. Subsequent advancements focused on text-image alignment, with DALL-E 3 [6] leveraging recaptioned datasets for superior prompt adherence. Furthermore, the architectural shift from U-Nets [108] to transformers [125], pioneered by DiT [97], unlocked unprecedented scalability. This transformerdriven scalability is prominently demonstrated by the spatiotemporal video generation of Sora [12] and the rectified flow formulations of Stable Diffusion 3 [36] for complex image synthesis. Most recently, this trajectory has culminated in the proprietary and closed-source Nano Banana series [43] and ChatGPT Images series [94], which currently define the state-of-the-art in high-fidelity text-to-image generation. Controllable Generation and Editing. Relying solely on text prompts often falls short when precise spatial control is required. To address this, ControlNet [141] introduced auxiliary conditioning, enabling diffusion models to respect spatial hints (e.g., depth or poses) without compromising the original generative prior. In the domain of image editing, InstructPix2Pix [11] pioneered instruction-driven modifications, allowing users to alter existing images via natural language instructions without relying on complex manual masks. Vision-Language Models (VLMs). Driven by the success of LLMs, VLMs have rapidly advanced visual comprehension. Pioneering works like LLaVA [80] aligned visual encoderspredominantly based on Vision Transformers (ViTs) [34]with LLMs via visual instruction tuning. Recent state-of-the-art models, such as Ovis [87] and the Qwen-VL series [3, 4], have further refined structural alignment and visual reasoning, enabling the processing of high-resolution inputs for intricate multimodal tasks. Towards Unified Multimodal Generation. Building upon VLMs reasoning and diffusion models generative prowess, the community is shifting towards consolidated architectures. OmniGen [132] and OmniGen2 [131] pioneered this by natively handling diverse generation and editing tasks within single framework. Concurrently, while proprietary models like Googles Nano Banana series [43] have introduced reasoning-guided synthesis via interleaved prompts, open-weight alternatives such as the Qwen-Image series [130] and Z-Image [138] integrate deep visual understanding with scalable, efficient generation, paving the way for versatile and intuitive multimodal AI systems. B.2 Macro-Architecture Design Empirical observations of state-of-the-art proprietary models, such as ChatGPT Images series [94] and the Nano Banana series [43], reveal consistent latency prior to generation. We hypothesize that, regardless of their underlying architectures, these systems employ robust comprehension module to meticulously analyze and deconstruct user instructions, which naturally incurs temporal overhead. Motivated by this, our preliminary architectural design utilized massive 72B Vision-Language Model (VLM) to drive 10B Diffusion Transformer (DiT), where the VLM was kept frozen and only the DiT parameters were updated. However, this configuration imposes prohibitive computational overheads during both training and inference, rendering it inaccessible to the broader research community. To address this, we propose decoupled, compute-efficient paradigm: we employ moderately large VLM (e.g., 32B) as an Instruction Reasoner for complex logic comprehension, paired with lightweight VLM (e.g., 8B) acting as an Instruction Encoder to extract hidden states as conditioning signals for the DiT. This decoupled strategy significantly mitigates both training and inference costs compared to relying on monolithic, large VLM. 56 Figure 34 Illustration of the Instruction Reasoner workflow. The input text instruction, formatted via predefined chat template with system prompt, is processed by the Reasoner. In image editing scenarios, reference image is appended to the input. The Reasoner yields comprehensively analyzed, deconstructed, and rewritten instruction to condition the subsequent generation pipeline. B.2.

sections.title: Instruction Reasoner
sections.content: The operational pipeline of the Instruction Reasoner is depicted in Figure 34. We introduce unified architecture that seamlessly integrates both text-to-image (T2I) generation and image editing. Specifically, the system determines the task modality based on the presence of visual inputs: if the user-provided reference image is omitted, the process defaults to T2I task and applies the corresponding T2I system prompt. Conversely, the inclusion of reference image explicitly designates an editing operation, thereby activating an editing-specific system prompt. Ultimately, the Reasoner outputs meticulously parsed, structurally decomposed, and refined textual prompt, which serves as the optimized conditioning signal for the subsequent generation stages. B.2.2 Instruction Encoder and Image Generation Upon acquiring the text instructions that have been analyzed, deconstructed, and rewritten by the Instruction Reasoner, we employ subsequent pipeline to generate images. This pipeline primarily consists of two core modules: an Autoregressive Transformer [125] and Diffusion Transformer (DiT) [97]. The Autoregressive Transformer essentially functions as Vision-Language Model (VLM), serving as our Instruction Encoder. The sub-modules comprising the pipeline of the Instruction Encoder are briefly introduced as follows (see Figure.35): Text Tokenizer and Embedding Table This module is responsible for tokenizing the rewritten text instructions and projecting them into sequence of embedding vectors. This sequence serves as partial input to the Autoregressive Transformer. Vision Transformer (ViT) The number of user-provided reference images can range from zero to multiple. When present, the Vision Transformer (ViT) [34] encodes them into the same latent space as the text prompt embeddings. These visual and textual embeddings are concatenated into unified sequence before being fed into the Autoregressive Transformer. As standard component of most VLMs, the ViT processes these reference images, which are here downsampled to 384 384 resolution to prevent excessively long vector sequences. Notably, if no reference images are provided, the task is defined as pure text-to-image generation, and the ViT is consequently bypassed. System Prompt The task paradigm is determined based on the presence of reference images. Specifically, the absence of any reference images defines text-to-image generation task, whereas the presence of one or more reference images indicates an image editing task. The system prompt is adaptively formulated corresponding to the specific task type. Prompt Tuning Transformer (Optional) This is highly lightweight, trainable Transformer module. In our implementation, it is fixed at 3 layers, with the architecture of each layer illustrated in Figure 37. It contains only 32 trainable prompt token embeddings and employs causal masking. The output from its final layer is prepended to the input sequence of the Instruction Encoder. The Prompt Tuning Transformer serves as an optional trainable module designed to enhance the instruction parsing and comprehension capabilities of the overall text-to-image model. Since the Instruction Encoder remains frozen during training, mathematically, the introduction of this module globally modulates the embedding distribution of the hidden state sequence output by the encoder, thereby facilitating better alignment with the semantic space required by the DiT. 57 Figure 35 Pipeline of the instruction encoder and DiT. The framework defaults to image editing when reference image is provided, and text-to-image generation otherwise. thumbnail of the reference image is fed to the Instruction Encoder to aid comprehension, while the original image (scaled within the resolution limit) is processed by the DiT. The system prompt adaptively adjusts to the specific task. Furthermore, an optional trainable Prompt Tuning Transformer is introduced to enhance instruction parsing. Note that the input user instructions have been pre-analyzed and rewritten by the Instruction Reasoner. It is worth noting that the Prompt Tuning Transformer is an optional module, which will be discussed further in the subsequent experimental section. In addition, Figure 35(b) illustrates the Diffusion Transformer (DiT) component. The sub-modules involved in this component are briefly introduced below: Variational Autoencoder (VAE) When user-provided reference images are present, to balance performance and computational efficiency, we first employ Variational Autoencoder (VAE) [67] encoder to map them into latent space before feeding them into the DiT. Consequently, our diffusion training is conducted entirely within this latent space [107]. Once mapped, the reference images undergo patchification, followed by processing through an Embedder and Refiner. The resulting hidden states, along with the hidden states corresponding to the noise, are fed into the DiT layers for sequential processing. The output of the final layer must be unpatchified and passed through the VAE decoder to reconstruct the output image. When no reference images are provided, the VAE encoder is bypassed; however, the VAE decoder remains essential for decoding the final output image, as the noise depicted in the bottom right of Figure 35(b) essentially represents the noise state within the VAE-encoded latent space. Embedder and Refiner The Embedder is standard linear layer designed to project hidden states from one latent space to another. The Refiner is simple, lightweight Transformer [125]. By default, it consists of only 2 layers, with each layers architecture depicted in Figure 37 (identical to the structure of the Prompt Tuning Transformer). Compared to the Embedder, the Refiner also accounts for the inter-relationships among elements in the newly patchified sequence. Notably, the user instructions, reference images, and noise each possess their own independent Embedders and Refiners. Single-Stream and Dual-Stream Layers Our DiT employs stacked architecture comprising Nd dualstream layers followed by Ns single-stream layers. After being projected into unified latent space by their respective Refiners and associated modules, the reference images and noise are concatenated and fed into the image stream of the dual-stream layers. Simultaneously, the hidden states of the user instructions from the final layer of the Instruction Encoder are projected into the instruction latent space by their corresponding 58 Refiner, etc., and then input into the instruction stream of the dual-stream layers. The outputs from the two streams of the final dual-stream layer are concatenated and fed into the unified stream of the single-stream layers. Following series of operations, the output from the single-stream layers is ultimately decoded by the VAE decoder to produce the final image. Figure 35 illustrates the macro-architecture design and overall pipeline for text-to-image generation and image editing. During the actual inference phase, the DiT component typically requires multiple iterative steps to synthesize high-fidelity images [56, 97]. This iterative process is accompanied by the transition of the time step from 0 to 1 (or from 1 to 0, depending on the specific formulation), along with corresponding updates to the Time Embedding. For the specific iterative sampling algorithm during generation, one can employ the standard first-order Euler method commonly utilized in Flow Matching [79], or fast dedicated ODE solvers such as DPM-Solver++ [86]. B.3 Micro-Architecture Details Building upon the macro-level overview presented in the previous section, we now delve into the microarchitectural details of our model. Our specific design draws inspiration from recent foundational works [24, 29, 36, 38, 79, 97, 120, 127, 130132, 147], which we have appropriately modified and extended to suit our objectives. Overall, we propose unified framework that seamlessly supports both text-to-image generation and image editing. By integrating and adapting the aforementioned designs, we incorporate specialized modules to enhance fine-grained image comprehension and comprehensive multimodal alignment. The detailed components are elaborated below. B.3.1 Dual-Stream Layer The architecture of the dual-stream layer is illustrated in Figure 36(a). In this design, the user instruction stream and the image stream (comprising the noisy latent and the optional reference image) are processed separately within the layer. Unlike conventional dual-stream architectures, which typically either maintain independent linear projections solely for the Query, Key, and Value tensors of the two streams [126], or decouple only small subset of additional computational modules [36], our approach draws inspiration from recent advancements [38, 127, 147]. Specifically, it incorporates more comprehensive set of independent intra-stream and inter-stream modules to facilitate deeper computational processing and feature interaction. Boosting Multimodal Understanding Profound comprehension of user instructions is crucial for image generation and editing. Our VLM-based Instruction Reasoner deconstructs user prompts, feeding rewritten text instructions to the Instruction Encoder to extract generation conditions. During this process, although this encoder utilizes ViT to process optional reference images, its primary focus remains text instruction modeling rather than comprehensive multimodal understanding. To explicitly enhance multimodal understanding, following recent architectures [38, 97, 127, 130, 147], we adopt an independent attention module for the image modality (comprising the noisy latent and optional reference image), as shown in Figure 36(a, right). Within the dual-stream layer, the right stream processes image token interactions to deepen visual comprehension. Conversely, the left stream fuses the instruction and image modalities (Figure 36(a, left)). By incorporating instruction residual connections, it inherently emphasizes instruction modeling from holistic multimodal perspective, despite performing full-modality attention. Subsequently, the image information derived from the left streams full-modality analysis is fused with the residual and internal self-attention outputs of the right stream. This integration yields refined representation grounded in holistic comprehension while prioritizing the image modality. Furthermore, the dual-stream layer features combination of shared and independent computational modules, including three independent Query, Key, and Value (QKV) projections. These QKV sets are allocated to: the text modality, the image modality used for holistic enhancement, and the image modalitys internal self-attention, respectively. Collectively, these two streamsrooted in holistic understanding but respectively emphasizing the instruction and image modalitiesenhance the models multimodal comprehension at fine-grained level. This synergy paves the way for more macroscopic and comprehensive modeling in the subsequent single-stream layers after the streams converge. Figure 36 Detailed architecture of the dual-stream and single-stream layers. Bold lines indicate the Main Path constructed via residual connections, while thin lines represent Branch Paths involving complex computations. Retaining only the bold lines reduces both layers to identity mappings. The branch paths merge into the main path at appropriate junctures to influence the parameter distribution of layers output. Main Path, Residue, and Modulation Our dual-stream layer adopts the classic sandwich norm [32, 147] and the modulation mechanism [97] to facilitate information fusion and signal control. However, our specific implementation introduces slight modifications (see Figure 36(a)). The multi-norm design significantly enhances model stability during pre-training with large learning rates, thereby accelerating convergence and improving overall robustness. In Figure 36(a), the bold lines denote the Main Path, which is fundamentally constructed with residual connections to stabilize the parameter distribution across layers. If only this main path existed, layer reduces to an identity mapping. Conversely, the thin lines represent Branch Paths that incorporate various operations, including attention and modulation. These branches are responsible for analyzing and processing the input representations. The computational results from these branch paths are subsequently merged into the main path at appropriate junctures, ultimately shaping the final output distribution of the layer. 60 B.3.2 Single-Stream Layer The architecture of our single-stream layer follows numerous classic designs [38, 127, 130, 131, 147]. Specifically, we adopt the classical modulation mechanism [97] and sandwich norm [32, 147] (see Figure 36(a)). This layer merges the hidden states of both the instruction and image streams from the final dual-stream layerwhich have already been deeply comprehended and modeledinto unified representation, and then it performs comprehensive multi-modal fusion and analysis, processing the sequence layer by layer to yield the final output. The computational logic within this layer is unified and modality-agnostic; both instruction and image inputs share the same parameters, including the QKV projections. As in the dual-stream layer, the bold lines denote the main path, while the thin lines represent the branch paths. Retaining only the main path reduces the layer to an identity mapping. The branch paths are responsible for complex computations and merge into the main path at appropriate junctures. B.4 Summary of Architectural Design In this section, we summarize our architectural design and detail the specific hyperparameter choices. Specifically, our DiT architecture comprises Ns = 32 single-stream layers and Nd = 8 dual-stream layers. We incorporate 3D Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) (following [121, 131]) and, by default, utilize the hidden states from the final layer of the Instruction Encoder as the condition for the DiT. The base DiT model contains 10,292.56M (10B) trainable parameters. When the optional Prompt Tuning Transformer is included, the total trainable parameter count slightly increases to 11,022.55M. Further architectural details and hyperparameter settings are comprehensively summarized in Table 10. The complete training process is summarized in Table 11. The parameters Max Output Pixels, Max Input Pixels, and Max Side Length define the spatial constraints during training. During data preprocessing, images exceeding these thresholds are dynamically downscaled to fit within the limits, while smaller or equal-sized images remain unchanged, inherently enabling mixed-resolution training. For the TI2I training phase, we construct mixed dataset of approximately 22M samples. This comprises 11.29M pure T2I samples sampled from the 47.19M Boogu Syllabus dataset (see 3.2.2), and 10.70M editing samples augmented from our initially constructed 2.50M TI2I dataset. Consequently, our image editing model is optimized through combination of mixed-resolution and mixed-task training. sections.title: C Boosted Orthogonal Guidance
sections.content: Classifier-free guidance [55] and related inference-time steering techniques have become indispensable for improving prompt adherence in text-to-image DiTs. However, aggressively amplifying guidance often trades alignment for degraded appearance, manifesting as oversaturation, flattened tones, over-smoothing, and visually implausible artifacts. In this section, we introduce Boosted Orthogonal Guidance (BOG), new training-free inference method for DiT-based text-to-image generation that targets this failure mode while explicitly enhancing fine-grained details and cinematic atmosphere. Inspired by the key insight behind APG [109] namely that guidance updates can be meaningfully decomposed into components parallel and orthogonal to the models predicted flow BOG emphasizes the informationpreserving orthogonal contribution to avoid color blow-up and texture collapse. Crucially, BOG departs from the conventional view that DiT prediction is merely flattened high-dimensional vector: we treat each per-step predicted tensor as 2D matrix aligned with the images inherent spatial structure, echoing the matrix-aware perspective adopted in optimizer design (e.g., Moun-style [63] updates). Building on this view, BOG applies an efficient orthogonalization approximation to the predicted matrix at every denoising step, acting as structured normalization that not only controls magnitude but also enriches the diversity of row/column directions. Unlike vector-norm rescaling which preserves single global direction while adjusting only scale our matrix-structured normalization encourages higher effective rank, thereby retaining more informative variation across spatial dimensions. Empirically, this rank-preserving, orthogonality-boosted guidance leads to richer micro-structures, improved tonal depth, and more coherent film-like lighting and ambience, while mitigating oversaturation without introducing additional training or meaningful sampling overhead. C.1 Matrix-Structured View of DiT Predictions Standard inference in DiTs typically treats the model prediction at each flow-matching (or denoising) step as single flattened 1D update direction in high-dimensional state space: under conditional or unconditional prompting, the current latent state (often noisy or partially denoised image representation) is moved along this vector field toward corresponding clean-image hidden state [56, 79, 8486, 115, 117, 119]. While this viewpoint is mathematically convenient, it implicitly discards key inductive bias of the image modality namely, that the predicted hidden state is inherently spatial. Concretely, the DiT output is tensor of shape [B, C, H, ]; after ignoring batch and channel dimensions, the remaining [H, ] structure is not an arbitrary reshaping artifact but meaningful 2D grid aligned with the image plane. This mismatch becomes more pronounced in how normalization is commonly applied: prevailing norm or rescaling strategies first collapse the 2D prediction into 1D vector and then rescale its Euclidean length, which changes only the magnitude in the vector field while leaving the update direction (and its spatial composition) essentially untouched. Others additionally subtract mean term either the batch mean or the per-sample mean over features thereby performing mean-centering. As result, such vector-level normalization still overlooks spatial organization that could be leveraged to preserve informative variation and avoid overly smooth updates. Motivated by recent optimizer designs that explicitly respect the matrix/tensor structure rather than reducing updates to flat vectors [50, 63], we instead adopt 2D normalization strategy that operates directly on the matrix-structured prediction at each step. C.1.2 Matrix Orthogonal Decomposition Preliminary - Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) We briefly review key concepts in diffusion models and introduce the notation used in this paper. pdata(x) denotes sample, and here we let xt = α(t)x + σ(t)ϵ with ϵ (0, I), which describes the forward noising process in diffusion models. Here σ(t) with [0, 1] gives the noise shedule, i.e., the amount of noise at the time step t. Following [131, 147], we adopt the setting σ(1) = 0 and σ(0) = σmax, so that = 0 corresponds to (approximately) pure noise and = 1 corresponds to clean sample. Building upon the Probability Flow ODE (PF-ODE) framework [65, 119], the forward diffusion process can be equivalently described by deterministic ODE that preserves the marginal probability densities. From this continuous-time perspective, the generative process simply amounts to solving this empirical ODE to map pure noise distribution to the clean data manifold, which is formulated as: dxt = σ(t)xtlog pt(xt)dσ(t) = σ(t)σ(t)xt log pt(xt)dt #1 (5) where pt(xt) represents the marginal distribution of the perturbed data at time t, bridging the Gaussian noise maxI) and the target data distribution p1 = pdata. Generative sampling is performed by numerically p0 = (0, σ2 integrating this ODE forward from = 0 to = 1. To operationalize this process, the analytically intractable score xtlog pt(xt) is approximated by Diffusion Transformer (DiT) Dθ(xt, t), which is optimized to recover the uncorrupted sample from the noisy observation xt. For conditional generation, the DiT architecture is readily augmented to Dθ(xt, t, c), enabling auxiliary inputs c, such as text instructions or class labels, to guide the generation. # For our setting σ(1) = 0 and σ(0) = σmax, σ(t) 0 is monotonically decreasing function with σ(t) 0, and dt 0 during generation. 65 Preliminary - Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) CFG [55] is widely adopted inference technique that enhances sample quality and condition alignment by extrapolating between conditional and unconditional model predictions. Given null condition0 c, CFG modifies the output of the DiT denoiser at each sampling time step as follows: DCF G(xt, t, c) = Dθ(xt, t, c) + ω (Dθ(xt, t, c) Dθ(xt, t, c)) = Dθ(xt, t, c) + (ω 1)Dθ,xt,t (6) where Dθ,xt,t = Dθ(xt, t, c) Dθ(xt, t, c). The DiT output is typically tensor of shape [B, C, H, ]; in some cases for simplicity, we can omit the batch size and channel dimension C, treating it as an [H, ] matrix. The unconditional prediction Dθ(xt, t, c) is conventionally obtained by randomly dropping the condition during training, though separate unconditional DiT denoiser can also be trained for this purpose [66]. Similar to the truncation trick in GANs [10], CFG improves image quality at the expense of generation diversity [88]. While larger guidance scale ω generally yields better instruction alignment, recent studies indicate that at high ω values, the parallel component of Dθ,xt,t with respect to Dθ(xt, t, c) (when flattened into 1D vector) leads to image over-saturation [109]. They argue that this pushes the model away from previous CFG update directions and encourages it to focus more on the current one. However, our empirical observations indicate that setting ρ < 0 causes directional jitter for D(i) within the vector field across iterations. C.3 Putting It All Together We now present the complete pipeline of our proposed method. Algorithm 1 summarizes the computation of the Boosted Orthogonal Guidance (BOG) at single inference iteration step i. By explicitly treating the predicted tensors as spatial matrices, BOG systematically refines the guidance direction. It first smooths the trajectory using momentum, then normalizes the matrix to preserve spatial rank, and finally isolates the orthogonal componentseffectively diminishing the projections parallel to the conditional predictionto prevent oversaturation. Ultimately, this rank-preserving and orthogonality-boosted approach significantly elevates the visual quality, producing richer micro-details, enhanced tonal depth, and realistic cinematic lighting, all achieved zero-shot without meaningful overhead. . Specifically, it can increase the probability of structural distortions and reduce the success rate of text rendering. Consequently, we treat BOG as an optional module, akin to selectable filter. To mitigate these issues, we introduce simple hyperparameter: the BOG Interval (BOG). During inference, BOG is applied every BOG diffusion steps, while standard CFG is used for the remaining steps. For instance, in 10-step denoising process with BOG = 2, BOG is executed on even steps (2, 4, 6, 8, 10) and standard CFG on odd steps. Thus, BOG = 1 denotes full BOG inference. We recommend BOG = 2 as the default setting, which significantly alleviates the aforementioned artifacts, albeit with slight trade-off in the enhanced BOG-styled photographic texture. 67 C.4 Optimal Semi-Orthogonal Approximation Proof.
